---
title: "3.5 Methods: How to Write Them"
subtitle: "Part 1: Method Basics"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit3-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Methods: How to Write Them

### Topic intro

In object-oriented programming, the three main parts of a class are:

::: {.tight-list}
- the **instance variables**, which hold attributes or values associated with each object
- the **constructors**, whose job is to initialize the instance variables
- the **methods**, which contain the code for the behaviors of an object and can use the instance variables defined in the class
:::

## Unit 1 Review

### Turtle and static methods

In Unit 1, we used `Turtle` objects and called methods like `forward`, which changed the `x` and `y` coordinates, the instance variables, of the turtle.

We also defined static methods that did not work with objects.

In this unit, we will learn how to write our own methods in our own classes.

## Defining and Calling Methods

### Method definition

A **method** is a block of code that performs a specific task.

Methods are defined inside a class and can access the instance variables of the class.

Methods are usually `public`.

## Person `print` Method

### Example method definition and call

```java
public class Person
{
    // instance variables
    private String name;
    private String email;

    // Method definition: uses instance variables name and email
    public void print()
    {
        System.out.println("Name: " + name);
        System.out.println("Email: " + email);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        Person p = new Person();
        p.print();  // Method call
    }
}
```

## Three Steps

### Creating and calling a method

There are three steps to creating and calling a method:

::: {.tight-list}
- **Object of the Class**
- **Method Call**
- **Method Definition**
:::

## Step 1

### Object of the class

Declare an object of your class in the `main` method or from outside the class.

```java
// Step 1: declare an object in main or from outside the class
Classname objectName = new Classname();
```

## Step 2

### Method call

Whenever you want to use the method, call:

```java
// Step 2: call the object's method
objectName.methodName(); //Step 2
```

## Step 3

### Method definition

Write the method's **header** and **body** code.

```java
// Step 3: Define the method in the class
// method header
public void methodName()
{
      // method body for the code
}
```

## Calling Methods Flowchart {.image-fit}

### Static, instance, and same-class calls

![Comparing method calls to static and instance methods](../../assets/3-class-creation/3-5-methods-how-to-write-them/FlowChartCallingMethods.png){fig-align="center" width="76%"}

Class methods are called using the class name.

Instance methods are called using an object of the class.

## Instance Method Calls

### From outside the class

If you are calling the instance method from the `main` method or from another class, you must first create an object of that class.

Then call its methods using:

```java
object.methodName();
```

## Same-Class Method Calls

### From within the same class

If you are calling the method from within the same class, you can just call:

```java
methodName();
```

This will refer to the current object.

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: PersonPrint`

Try the following code.

Add a `print()` method in the `Person` class that prints out all the attributes of a person object:

::: {.tight-list}
- `name`
- `email`
- `phonenumber`
:::

## PersonPrint Starter

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class Person
{
    // instance variables
    private String name;
    private String email;
    private String phonenumber;

    // Constructor
    public Person(String n, String e, String p)
    {
        name = n;
        email = e;
        phonenumber = p;
    }

    // TODO: Add a print() method that prints out all the attributes (name, email, phonenumber)


    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        Person p1 = new Person("Taylor", "swift@gmail.com", "123-456-7890");
        p1.print();
    }
}
```
:::

## PersonPrint Test Target

### Runestone check

The Runestone test checks that your code contains this method header:

```java
public void print()
```

Keep the starter context: the constructor and `main` method are already provided.

## `void` Methods

### Performs an action, returns no value

A **void method** is a method that does not return a value.

It is used when you want to perform an action but do not need to return a value.

```java
public void methodName()
{
    // method body
}
```

## `print` Is `void`

### Output is the action

The `print` method above is a `void` method.

It does not return a value, but it does print out the name and email of the person.

In later sections, we will learn about setter methods that are also `void` methods.

## Non-`void` Methods

### Returns one value

A **non-void method** is a method that returns a single value.

Its body must have a **return** statement, usually at the end, that returns a variable's value or an expression.

Its header includes the **return type**, the type of the value in the return statement, in place of the keyword `void`.

## Non-`void` Method Shape

### Return type and return statement

```java
public returnType methodName()
{
    // method body
    return value;
}
```

In Unit 1, we used static non-void methods like `Math.random()`, which returned a random number.

We also used Turtle methods like `getXPos()` and `getYPos()`.

## Return by Value

### Flow of control

In non-void methods, a return expression compatible with the return type is evaluated, and the value is returned.

This is referred to as **return by value**.

The `return` keyword is used to return the flow of control to the point where the method or constructor was called.

## Return Statement Caution

### Code after return

Any code that is sequentially after a return statement will never be executed.

Usually non-void methods end with a return statement.

Executing a return statement inside a selection or iteration statement will halt the statement and exit the method or constructor.

## Common Errors

### Non-void methods

Some common errors when writing and using non-void methods are:

::: {.tight-list}
- forgetting a return type like `int` before the method name
- forgetting to use the `return` keyword to return a value at the end of the method
- returning too soon from a method
- forgetting to do something with the value returned from a method, like assigning it to a variable or printing it out
:::

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- identify the three class parts: instance variables, constructors, and methods
- distinguish a method definition from a method call
- create an object before calling an instance method from `main` or another class
- write the method header and body inside the class
- preserve the `PersonPrint` starter and add a `public void print()` method
- explain the difference between `void` and non-`void` methods
- explain why non-`void` methods need a compatible `return` value
:::

## End

### 3.5 Part 1 complete

Next: Accessors, getters, and `toString`.
